nodes.label: AI Fundamentals
nodes.desc: Foundation of artificial intelligence concepts
nodes.content:
## AI Fundamentals

Core concepts that underpin modern AI systems.

### Key Areas
- Machine Learning basics
- Neural Networks
- Training vs. Inference
- Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning
- Evaluation metrics and benchmarks

timeline.title: Course created
timeline.desc: Initial course structure defined.

nodes.label: Large Language Models
nodes.desc: Understanding LLMs - architecture, capabilities, limitations
nodes.content:
## Large Language Models

LLMs are neural networks trained on massive text datasets to understand and generate human language.

### Key Concepts
- **Transformer architecture** - attention mechanism, positional encoding
- **Training** - pre-training, fine-tuning, RLHF
- **Tokens and context windows** - how text is processed
- **Capabilities** - reasoning, code generation, summarization
- **Limitations** - hallucination, knowledge cutoff, context limits
- **Model families** - GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama

### Context Windows
| Model | Context |
|-------|--------|
| Claude Opus 4.6 | 200K tokens |
| GPT-4.5 | 128K tokens |
| Gemini 2 | 1M+ tokens |

nodes.label: AI Agents
nodes.desc: Autonomous AI systems that reason, plan, and use tools
nodes.content:
## AI Agents

Agents are AI systems that can autonomously reason, plan, and take actions to accomplish goals.

### What Makes an Agent
- **Reasoning** - can analyze problems and plan solutions
- **Tool use** - can invoke external tools and APIs
- **Memory** - maintains state across interactions
- **Autonomy** - can initiate actions independently
- **Multi-step** - executes complex workflows

### Agent Architecture
```
User Goal -> Agent (LLM + Planning) -> Tool Calls -> Results -> Next Step -> ... -> Final Output
```

### Examples
- Claude Code (coding agent)
- Devin (software engineering)
- AutoGPT, CrewAI (general purpose)

nodes.label: MCP Protocol
nodes.desc: Model Context Protocol - standard for agent-to-tool communication
nodes.content:
## Model Context Protocol (MCP)

Open standard that defines how AI agents interact with tools and resources. Think of it as **USB-C for AI**.

### Architecture
```
Host (App) <-> MCP Client <-> MCP Server
              (JSON-RPC)     (tools, resources)
```

### Three Primitives
| Primitive | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| **Tools** | Functions the model can call | `search_db(query)` |
| **Resources** | Data the model can read | `file://config.json` |
| **Prompts** | Reusable templates | "Summarize PR" |

### Key Properties
- JSON-RPC 2.0 communication
- Server discovery and capability negotiation
- One host can connect to multiple servers
- Model decides when to invoke tools

nodes.label: Server Instructions
nodes.desc: Injecting guidance into LLM system prompts for better tool use
nodes.content:
## MCP Server Instructions

Server instructions let MCP servers inject guidance into the LLM system prompt.

### When to Use
- Cross-tool dependencies and sequencing
- Rate limits and operational constraints
- Multi-step workflow patterns
- Conditional fallback behaviors

### Measured Impact (40 sessions)
- **With instructions:** 85% optimal workflow
- **Without:** 60% optimal workflow

### Best Practices
```
DO: Capture tool relationships, specify constraints
DON'T: Repeat tool descriptions, add marketing
```

**Critical rule:** "No instructions are better than poorly written instructions."

timeline.title: Blog post published
timeline.desc: Detailed guide with measured results from GitHub PR review testing.

nodes.label: MCP Bundles (.mcpb)
nodes.desc: Distribution packages for MCP servers - single-click install
nodes.content:
## MCP Bundles (.mcpb)

ZIP archives containing an MCP server + metadata for single-click installation.

### Bundle Structure
```
my-server.mcpb
  +-- manifest.json    (required)
  +-- server/          (Node.js, Python, or binary)
  +-- node_modules/    (dependencies)
  +-- icon.png         (optional)
```

### Benefits
- **Cross-client** - works in any MCP host
- **One-click install** - no manual config
- **Open-source** - `mcpb` CLI for creating bundles

timeline.title: Moved to MCP project
timeline.desc: Originally developed for Claude Desktop, now open-source under MCP project.

nodes.label: Agent Skills
nodes.desc: Rich workflow instructions that teach agents how to do complex tasks
nodes.content:
## Agent Skills

Folders of instructions, scripts, and resources that agents load on demand to perform tasks accurately.

### Why Skills?
Tools tell agents **what they can do**. Skills tell agents **how to accomplish workflows**.

### SKILL.md Format
```yaml
---
name: quarterly-analysis
description: Generates quarterly reports...
metadata:
  author: finance-team
  version: "1.0"
---
```

### Progressive Disclosure
1. **Metadata** (~100 tokens) - loaded at startup
2. **Instructions** (<5000 tokens) - loaded when activated
3. **Resources** (on demand) - scripts, references

### Adopted By
Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code, Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex, 25+ more

nodes.label: Skills Over MCP
nodes.desc: Active initiative exploring how skills are discovered and consumed through MCP
nodes.content:
## Skills Over MCP Interest Group

### Six Approaches
1. **First-Class Primitive (SEP-2076)** - `skills/list`, `skills/get`
2. **Registry Metadata** - skill refs in registry entries
3. **Skills as Tools/Resources** - `skill://` URI scheme
4. **Gateway Pattern** - wrapper composing multiple MCPs
5. **Server Instructions Ref** - pointer to skill resources
6. **Official Convention** - documented patterns, no protocol changes

### Key Findings
- Agents don't naturally discover skills
- Adherence decays over time as context grows
- `skill://` resource colocation works well

### Open Questions
- Convention vs. protocol extension?
- Who controls loading (model vs. app)?
- Multi-server dependencies?
- Security and trust model?

nodes.label: A2A Protocol
nodes.desc: Agent-to-Agent protocol for peer collaboration between autonomous agents
nodes.content:
## Agent2Agent Protocol (A2A)

Enables independent AI agents to collaborate as peers.

### Core Capabilities
1. **Discovery** - agents find each other
2. **Task Negotiation** - propose, accept, decline
3. **Shared Context** - pass rich context
4. **Complex Data Exchange** - files, artifacts
5. **Multi-turn Dialogue** - extended conversations

### Agent vs Tool Test
*"Does it require reasoning or multi-turn negotiation?"*
- **No** -> Tool (MCP)
- **Yes** -> Agent (A2A)

### Interaction Model
```
Client Agent --> Task Request --> Remote Agent
             <-- Clarifying Q  <--
             --> Context       -->
             <-- Final Result  <--
```

timeline.desc: Google announces the Agent-to-Agent protocol.

nodes.label: MCP + A2A Integration
nodes.desc: How MCP and A2A work together in layered architecture
nodes.content:
## MCP + A2A Together

```
A2A  = Agent-to-Agent (collaboration)
MCP  = Agent-to-Tool (capability access)
```

### Auto Repair Shop Example
| Interaction | Protocol |
|-------------|----------|
| Customer describes problem | A2A |
| Manager diagnoses | A2A |
| Mechanic runs scanner | **MCP** |
| Mechanic checks manual | **MCP** |
| Mechanic orders parts | A2A |

### Design Patterns
- **Hub and Spoke** - orchestrator delegates via A2A
- **Peer Network** - equals collaborate via A2A
- **Hierarchical** - layers decompose via A2A, execute via MCP

nodes.label: Agent UIs
nodes.desc: Technologies for agents to present rich visual interfaces
nodes.content:
## Agent User Interfaces

Agents need visual capabilities beyond text.

### The Problem
- Data viz returns JSON, not charts
- Form agent describes fields, not actual forms
- Dashboards as text tables

### Two Solutions
| Aspect | MCP Apps | A2UI |
|--------|----------|------|
| Format | HTML (iframe) | JSON |
| Rendering | Server controls | Client native |
| Security | Sandbox + review | Pre-approved catalog |
| Protocol | MCP | Agnostic |
| Framework | Web only | Any |

nodes.label: MCP Apps (SEP-1865)
nodes.desc: MCP extension for servers to deliver interactive HTML UIs to hosts
nodes.content:
## MCP Apps (SEP-1865)

Servers deliver **interactive HTML UIs** to host applications.

### How It Works
1. Register UI templates (`ui://` URI scheme)
2. Tool returns result + UI reference
3. Host renders in sandboxed iframe
4. Interactions via JSON-RPC postMessage

### Security (Defense-in-Depth)
- Iframe sandboxing
- Template pre-review
- Auditable JSON-RPC messaging
- User consent for tool invocations

### Key Decisions
- Templates pre-declared, not inline
- HTML-first (sandboxed iframes)
- Optional and backward-compatible
- Text fallbacks recommended

timeline.title: SEP-1865 published
timeline.desc: Collaboration between Anthropic, OpenAI, and MCP UI Community WG.

nodes.label: A2UI (Google)
nodes.desc: Declarative JSON format for agents to describe UI intent rendered natively
nodes.content:
## A2UI - Agent-to-User Interface

Agents describe UI **declaratively via JSON**. Clients render using native components.

### Core Principles
1. **Security-First** - intent, not code
2. **LLM-Optimized** - flat component list
3. **Framework-Agnostic** - Flutter, React, Angular
4. **Extensible** - Smart Wrappers

### Example
```json
{
  "components": [
    {"id":"chart1","type":"bar-chart","data":{...}},
    {"id":"btn1","type":"button","label":"Export"}
  ]
}
```
Client maps `bar-chart` -> native `<BarChart>` component.

nodes.label: MCP Authorization
nodes.desc: OAuth 2.1 based authorization for remote MCP servers
nodes.content:
## MCP Authorization (OAuth 2.1)

MCP provides transport-level authorization enabling clients to access protected remote servers on behalf of users.

### Key Concepts
- **OAuth 2.1 based** - uses PKCE, refresh token rotation, HTTPS-only
- **Protected Resource Metadata** (RFC 9728) - servers advertise their authorization server
- **Dynamic Client Registration** (RFC 7591) - clients register without prior relationship
- **Client ID Metadata Documents** - HTTPS URLs as client identifiers
- **Resource Indicators** (RFC 8707) - tokens bound to specific MCP servers

### Authorization Flow
```
1. Client -> MCP Server (no token)
2. Server -> 401 + WWW-Authenticate header
3. Client discovers auth server via Protected Resource Metadata
4. Client -> Auth Server (PKCE authorization code flow)
5. User authenticates and consents
6. Client receives access token
7. Client -> MCP Server (with Bearer token)
```

### Security Requirements
- PKCE mandatory (S256 method)
- Audience validation (servers MUST verify tokens are for them)
- Token passthrough forbidden
- Scope-based step-up authorization

### Three Registration Approaches
1. **Client ID Metadata Documents** (most common, no prior relationship)
2. **Pre-registration** (existing relationship)
3. **Dynamic Client Registration** (backwards compatibility)

nodes.label: Streamable HTTP
nodes.desc: Modern HTTP transport replacing SSE - supports remote MCP servers with sessions and resumability
nodes.content:
## Streamable HTTP Transport

Replaces the older HTTP+SSE transport (from protocol version 2024-11-05) with a more flexible approach.

### How It Works
- Client sends JSON-RPC messages as **HTTP POST** to a single MCP endpoint
- Server responds with either `application/json` (single response) or `text/event-stream` (SSE stream)
- Client can also **HTTP GET** the endpoint to open a server-initiated SSE stream

### Key Features
| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Session Management** | `Mcp-Session-Id` header for stateful sessions |
| **Resumability** | SSE event IDs + `Last-Event-ID` for reconnection |
| **Multiple Connections** | Client can have multiple SSE streams simultaneously |
| **Security** | Origin validation, localhost binding, authentication |

### vs. stdio Transport
```
stdio:           Local process, stdin/stdout, single client
Streamable HTTP:  Remote server, HTTP POST/SSE, multiple clients
```

### Session Lifecycle
1. Client POSTs `InitializeRequest`
2. Server returns `Mcp-Session-Id` header
3. Client includes session ID in all subsequent requests
4. Client sends HTTP DELETE to terminate session

### Protocol Version Header
All requests MUST include `MCP-Protocol-Version: 2025-06-18` header.

nodes.label: MCP Sampling
nodes.desc: Servers request LLM completions through the client - enables agentic server behavior
nodes.content:
## MCP Sampling

Allows MCP servers to request language model completions through the client, enabling agentic behaviors without the server needing its own LLM access.

### Why It Matters
- Server authors get LLM access without paying for or integrating an AI model
- Client maintains full control over permissions and security
- Separate context window from the main conversation

### How It Works
```
Server -> sampling/createMessage -> Client
Client -> (optional) User reviews request
Client -> LLM (forward request)
LLM -> Client (generation)
Client -> (optional) User reviews response
Client -> Server (approved response)
```

### Request Parameters
```json
{
  "messages": [...],
  "modelPreferences": {
    "hints": [{"name": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"}],
    "costPriority": 0.3,
    "speedPriority": 0.2,
    "intelligencePriority": 0.9
  },
  "systemPrompt": "You are a travel expert...",
  "maxTokens": 1500
}
```

### Human-in-the-Loop
- Users can review and modify requests before LLM processing
- Users can review and modify responses before returning to server
- Rate limiting and content validation by client

nodes.label: MCP Elicitation
nodes.desc: Servers request structured user input mid-flow via JSON Schema forms
nodes.content:
## MCP Elicitation

Enables servers to request specific information from users during interactions, creating dynamic workflows that adapt to user needs.

### The Problem It Solves
Without elicitation, servers must either:
- Require all information upfront
- Fail when data is missing

With elicitation, servers can pause and ask the user for input mid-operation.

### How It Works
```
Server -> elicitation/create -> Client
Client -> presents UI to user
User -> provides information
Client -> returns response to Server
Server -> continues processing
```

### Schema-Based Input
```json
{
  "method": "elicitation/requestInput",
  "params": {
    "message": "Confirm your booking details:",
    "schema": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "confirmBooking": { "type": "boolean" },
        "seatPreference": {
          "type": "string",
          "enum": ["window", "aisle", "no preference"]
        }
      },
      "required": ["confirmBooking"]
    }
  }
}
```

### Response Options
Users can:
- Provide the requested information
- Decline with optional explanation
- Cancel the entire operation

### Privacy
- Never requests passwords or API keys
- Clients warn about suspicious requests
- Users review data before sending

nodes.label: MCP Tasks
nodes.desc: Experimental durable execution wrappers for long-running MCP operations
nodes.content:
## MCP Tasks (Experimental)

Durable state machines that wrap MCP requests, enabling deferred result retrieval and status tracking for expensive or long-running operations.

### When to Use
- Expensive computations (data analysis, ML inference)
- Batch processing requests
- Multi-step operations with user input needed mid-flow
- Workflow automation

### Task Lifecycle
```
[*] -> working
working -> input_required | completed | failed | cancelled
input_required -> working | completed | failed | cancelled
completed/failed/cancelled -> [*]  (terminal states)
```

### Two-Phase Response Pattern
1. **Phase 1:** Server accepts request, returns `CreateTaskResult` with taskId immediately
2. **Phase 2:** Client polls `tasks/get`, then retrieves result via `tasks/result`

### Key Methods
| Method | Purpose |
|--------|--------|
| `tools/call` + `task` field | Create task-augmented request |
| `tasks/get` | Poll task status |
| `tasks/result` | Retrieve final result (blocks until terminal) |
| `tasks/list` | List all tasks (paginated) |
| `tasks/cancel` | Cancel a running task |

### Task Properties
- `taskId` - unique identifier
- `status` - working/input_required/completed/failed/cancelled
- `ttl` - time-to-live in milliseconds
- `pollInterval` - suggested polling frequency
- `createdAt` / `lastUpdatedAt` - ISO 8601 timestamps

### input_required Status
When server needs user input mid-task, it transitions to `input_required` and sends an elicitation request tagged with the task ID.

nodes.label: Structured Outputs
nodes.desc: JSON Schema validated tool outputs for reliable agent-to-tool data exchange
nodes.content:
## Structured Outputs in MCP

Tools can define an `outputSchema` (JSON Schema) so clients and LLMs can validate and parse results reliably.

### How It Works
Tools declare both `inputSchema` and `outputSchema`:
```json
{
  "name": "get_weather_data",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": { "location": { "type": "string" } }
  },
  "outputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "temperature": { "type": "number" },
      "conditions": { "type": "string" }
    },
    "required": ["temperature", "conditions"]
  }
}
```

### Dual Response Format
For backwards compatibility, tools return both:
- `content` - unstructured text (legacy)
- `structuredContent` - validated JSON object (new)

### Benefits
- **Schema validation** of responses
- **Type information** for programming language integration
- **Better LLM parsing** of returned data
- **Documentation** and developer experience

### Content Types in Tool Results
MCP tools can return rich content:
- **Text** - plain text responses
- **Images** - base64 encoded with MIME type
- **Audio** - base64 encoded audio data
- **Resource Links** - URIs to fetch later
- **Embedded Resources** - inline resource data

All content types support **annotations** with audience, priority, and modification timestamps.

timeline.title: Output schemas added
timeline.desc: Tools can now define outputSchema for validated structured results.

nodes.label: Agent Frameworks
nodes.desc: Development frameworks for building AI agents - LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Semantic Kernel
nodes.content:
## Agent Frameworks

Frameworks that provide structure for building AI agents with tool use, memory, and multi-step reasoning.

### Major Frameworks
| Framework | By | Focus |
|-----------|-----|-------|
| **LangGraph** | LangChain | Stateful, graph-based agent workflows |
| **CrewAI** | CrewAI | Multi-agent role-based collaboration |
| **AutoGen** | Microsoft | Multi-agent conversation patterns |
| **Semantic Kernel** | Microsoft | Enterprise AI orchestration (.NET/Python) |
| **PydanticAI** | Pydantic | Type-safe Python agents |
| **FastMCP** | Community | Pythonic MCP server framework |

### How They Relate to MCP/A2A
```
Framework provides:  Agent structure, memory, orchestration
MCP provides:        Standardized tool access
A2A provides:        Standardized agent-to-agent communication
```

Frameworks are increasingly adopting MCP:
- **LangGraph** - MCP client integration
- **PydanticAI** - native MCP tool support + skills via PR #3780
- **FastMCP 3.0** - native skills provider
- **Semantic Kernel** - MCP server connectivity

### Key Patterns
- **ReAct** - Reason + Act loop (think, use tool, observe, repeat)
- **Plan-and-Execute** - create plan first, then execute steps
- **Reflexion** - self-reflection and correction after errors
- **Chain of Thought** - step-by-step reasoning before action

timeline.desc: Major frameworks converge on MCP support.

nodes.label: Agent Security
nodes.desc: Security patterns for AI agents - prompt injection defense, sandboxing, trust models
nodes.content:
## Agent Security

Critical security considerations when building and deploying AI agents.

### Threat Categories
1. **Prompt Injection** - malicious instructions in tool outputs or user inputs
2. **Tool Misuse** - agents calling destructive tools without proper authorization
3. **Data Exfiltration** - sensitive data leaking through tool calls
4. **Confused Deputy** - agents acting on behalf of wrong principal

### MCP Security Model
- Tool annotations mark behavior (read-only, destructive, idempotent)
- Clients MUST treat annotations as untrusted from untrusted servers
- Human-in-the-loop for sensitive operations
- Show tool inputs to users before calling server
- Rate limiting on tool invocations

### Defense Layers
```
1. Input Validation    - sanitize all inputs at boundaries
2. Permission Model    - user consent for tool invocations
3. Sandboxing         - isolated execution environments
4. Audit Logging      - track all tool usage
5. Token Scoping      - least-privilege access tokens
```

### Tool Annotations (Hints)
- `readOnlyHint` - tool does not modify state
- `destructiveHint` - tool may perform destructive operations
- `idempotentHint` - safe to call multiple times
- `openWorldHint` - tool interacts with external entities

### Trust Hierarchy
Skills are untrusted documents, not directives. Trust should align with MCP server trust level. Clients MUST NOT auto-apply skills without explicit policy.

nodes.label: RAG Patterns
nodes.desc: Retrieval-Augmented Generation - connecting LLMs to external knowledge via MCP resources
nodes.content:
## Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

RAG enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant external data before generation. MCP Resources provide a standardized way to implement RAG.

### Core RAG Pipeline
```
Query -> Embed -> Search Vector DB -> Retrieve Chunks -> Augment Prompt -> Generate
```

### How RAG Maps to MCP
| RAG Component | MCP Primitive |
|---------------|---------------|
| Document store | Resources (`resources/list`, `resources/read`) |
| Search/retrieval | Tools (`search_documents(query)`) |
| Knowledge updates | Resource subscriptions (`resources/subscribe`) |
| Structured data | Resource templates (`docs://{collection}/{id}`) |

### Key Patterns
- **Naive RAG** - embed query, find similar chunks, augment prompt
- **Advanced RAG** - query rewriting, re-ranking, hybrid search
- **Agentic RAG** - agent decides what to retrieve and when
- **Graph RAG** - knowledge graph + vector search combined

### MCP Resource Features for RAG
- **Direct Resources** - fixed URIs to specific data
- **Resource Templates** - parameterized queries (`weather://forecast/{city}`)
- **Subscriptions** - real-time updates when data changes
- **MIME types** - proper content type handling
- **Annotations** - audience, priority, and modification timestamps

### Embedding Models
Common choices: OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere embed-v3, local models (sentence-transformers). Vector stores: Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, pgvector.

timeline.desc: Retrieval-augmented generation becomes standard agent architecture pattern.

nodes.label: Agent Memory
nodes.desc: How agents maintain state across sessions - context window, vector stores, knowledge graphs
nodes.content:
## Agent Memory & State

Agents need memory to be useful beyond single-turn interactions.

### Memory Types
| Type | Mechanism | Persistence |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Short-term** | Context window | Session only |
| **Long-term** | Vector DB / Knowledge Graph | Across sessions |
| **Episodic** | Stored interaction logs | Across sessions |
| **Procedural** | Skills / CLAUDE.md files | Permanent |

### Short-Term (Context Window)
- Conversation history within current context
- Limited by window size (200K tokens for Claude)
- "Lost in the middle" - attention degrades with length
- Strategy: summarize older messages to compress

### Long-Term (Vector DB)
```
Experience -> Embed -> Store in Vector DB
New query -> Embed -> Similarity search -> Retrieve relevant memories
```
This is RAG applied to the agent's own history.

### Knowledge Graphs
- Structured memory: entities + relationships
- Better than vectors for precise factual recall
- Can combine with vector search (hybrid)

### Episodic Memory
Recording specific episodes: what happened, actions taken, outcome.
Enables learning from successes and failures.
(Stanford "Generative Agents" paper, 2023)

### Procedural / Skill Memory
- Agent Skills (SKILL.md) as compiled procedural knowledge
- CLAUDE.md project files as persistent memory
- Reusable patterns saved for future tasks

### Architecture Patterns
- Read-write memory within sessions
- Reflection loops for consolidation
- Memory scoping (user / project / org level)
- Relevance decay for outdated information
